stages.user_goal: Establish the foundational details of the event to create a draft in the system.
actions: Selects 'Create event' from professional dashboard
Enters event name, type, dates, and times
Attempts to select organizer profile, clicks to create a new one
Fills out new organizer details (name, email, socials, logo) in an overlay modal
Enters and validates event location on a map
Uploads event poster image (fails once due to size, succeeds on retry)
Configures a basic first ticket (name, units, fee distribution, price, type)
Defines audience parameters (attendee count, location, level, dance styles)
stages.system_responsibilities: Generate the database record for the event, associate it with a geographic location and an organizer entity, and initialize a draft state. The system does NOT auto-save progress if the user abandons the modal.

decision_points.decision: Fee distribution model (Apply to client, Assume fee, Split)
decision_points.required_information: Understanding of how the platform's fees are calculated and how they impact final payout vs. perceived customer price.
decision_points.notes: [Inference] The user must decide how to handle fees without a real-time calculator on this specific screen showing the exact monetary impact.

decision_points.decision: Creating a new organizer vs. selecting an existing one
decision_points.required_information: Whether the event belongs to a new brand or an existing entity.
decision_points.notes: Interrupts the primary linear flow with a heavy sub-workflow.

friction_points: Hidden constraints: Image upload size requirements (minimum 500px) are only revealed after an upload fails, causing unnecessary rework.
Flow interruption: Creating an organizer profile is a lengthy process forced into a modal inside step 2 of a 6-step wizard. This breaks the user's mental model of simple event creation.
Premature complex decisions: Forcing the user to define fee structures and audience demographics during the initial 'quick' setup increases time-to-value.
implicit_assumptions: User has all marketing assets (logos, posters) ready before starting.
User understands the platform's specific terminology regarding fee splitting.
User intends to complete the 6-step wizard in a single session.
opportunity_areas: Allow bypassing organizer creation and image uploads during the initial draft phase.
Display image size and aspect ratio requirements proactively before the upload interaction.
Move audience demographic questions to a post-creation 'marketing profile' section.

stages.user_goal: Define complex pricing tiers, grouping, and purchasing rules for attendees.
actions: Navigates to 'Tickets' in the event dashboard
Duplicates an existing ticket to act as a template
Creates a 'Group' to visually organize tickets
Edits ticket prices, selecting 'Scaled by dates' or 'Scaled by sales'
Adds multiple price rows for dynamic pricing
Modifies ticket visibility and availability windows
Configures which data fields are required from the purchaser (Email, ID, Phone, etc.)
Reorders tickets using a drag-and-drop 'Change order' toggle
stages.system_responsibilities: Store discrete ticket entities, apply chronological or inventory-based triggers to adjust prices automatically, and enforce data collection rules during checkout.

decision_points.decision: Price scaling strategy (Fixed vs. Date-based vs. Sales-based)
decision_points.required_information: Anticipated demand curve and financial targets for the event.
decision_points.notes: Requires building a mental matrix of dates, capacities, and prices without a visual preview of the resulting pricing timeline.

decision_points.required_information: Legal or operational requirements for identifying attendees at the door.
decision_points.notes: Granular toggles (Full name, Email, Phone, Gender, etc.) per ticket require repetitive configuration if multiple ticket types exist.

friction_points: Overloaded interface: The ticket edit modal contains a massive amount of configuration hidden behind nested tabs (General, Prices, Texts, Advanced), making it easy to miss crucial settings.

Lack of visual validation: Setting up 'Scaled by dates' pricing requires the user to input date ranges and prices manually. There is no timeline graph to warn the user if they create a gap or overlap in their pricing dates.
Mode switching: Reordering tickets requires activating a specific 'Change order' mode, moving items, and then deactivating it, rather than just natively allowing drag-and-drop in the default view.
implicit_assumptions: User possesses an advanced mental model of yield management and dynamic pricing.
User remembers to replicate data collection requirements across every individual ticket they create.
opportunity_areas: Implement a visual timeline or capacity bar chart to preview how dynamic pricing will behave over time.
Allow global data collection settings (e.g., 'Require ID for all tickets') rather than forcing per-ticket configuration.
Enable inline editing for basic ticket properties (price, name) without opening a heavy modal.

stages.user_goal: Prevent overselling by capping the total available inventory across multiple ticket variants.
actions: Navigates to 'Stock control' section
Creates a new stock control entity
Sets a total unit limit
Selects multiple existing tickets to share this specific limit limit
stages.system_responsibilities: Monitor combined sales of linked tickets and automatically halt sales for all linked items when the master cap is reached.

decision_points.decision: Which tickets share a capacity pool
decision_points.required_information: Physical venue capacity constraints vs. individual ticket allocations.
decision_points.notes: User must abstractly link discrete items created in a previous step to a new mathematical constraint.

friction_points: Hidden dependencies: The relationship between 'Tickets' and 'Stock control' is disjointed. A user could easily create 5 ticket types, forget to navigate to the separate 'Stock control' tab, and accidentally oversell their venue by 5x.
Abstract mental model: Treating 'Stock' as a separate entity from 'Tickets' requires the user to understand relational database logic, rather than just saying 'Cap total event sales at X'.
implicit_assumptions: User understands that ticket unit limits and global stock limits are two separate variables that interact with each other.
User will actively monitor and remember to update stock controls if they add new ticket types later.
opportunity_areas: Integrate basic global capacity limits directly into the main 'Tickets' view.
Warn the user if the sum of individual ticket capacities exceeds a known maximum, rather than relying solely on abstract 'Stock controls'.

stages.user_goal: Configure incentives, hidden access, and trackable links to drive sales.
actions: Navigates to Discounts
Creates a discount code (Value, Type, Limit by uses, Limit by dates, applies to specific tickets)
Navigates to Private Sales
Creates a private sale link to hide specific tickets behind a custom URL
Views Promotions tab (paid advertising options)
stages.system_responsibilities: Generate unique URLs, apply mathematical deductions to checkout sessions based on code matching, and manage visibility states based on access links.

decision_points.required_information: Which specific tickets should be eligible for the discount.
decision_points.notes: User must manually map discounts to tickets using toggle switches.

decision_points.required_information: Expected ROI of the platform's proprietary advertising packages.
decision_points.notes: [Inference] Evaluated based on the pricing matrix shown (e.g., Promo Basic vs Promo Boost).

friction_points: Siloed promotional tools: 'Discounts', 'Private sales', and 'Promoters' are separated into entirely distinct workflows. Creating a campaign that uses elements of all three requires significant back-and-forth navigation.
Redundant ticket mapping: Just like stock control, the user must re-select which tickets apply to which discount or private link, relying heavily on memory of their ticket hierarchy.
implicit_assumptions: User runs structured marketing campaigns that cleanly fit into either 'public discount' or 'private link' paradigms.
User is willing to invest in the platform's internal advertising tools.
opportunity_areas: Unify 'Discounts' and 'Private Sales' into a single 'Campaigns' or 'Access Rules' interface.
Allow creating a discount directly from the ticket creation modal, rather than forcing navigation to a new module.

structural_patterns: Database-driven UI: The interface closely mirrors the underlying relational database structure (Event -> Ticket -> Stock Pool -> Discount). It forces the user to assemble the relationships manually.
Siloed Configuration: Major features (Pricing, Capacity, Marketing, Analytics) are strictly segregated by a heavy left-hand navigation menu.
Gamified Onboarding: The use of the 'Potential Index' acts as a structural pattern to encourage users to fill out more database fields to improve their listing quality.
core_weaknesses: Fragmented Capacity Management: Separating ticket creation from stock control creates a severe risk of accidental overselling.
High Interaction Cost for Basic Tasks: Simple edits (like reordering tickets or changing a price) require mode-switches or opening heavy modal windows.
Blind Configuration: The system asks for complex date/time/price matrices without providing visual feedback or timelines to verify the logic.
Redundant Data Entry: Applying settings (like attendee data requirements or discounts) to multiple tickets requires repetitive, granular clicking rather than bulk actions.
Delayed Error Validation: Important constraints (like image minimum sizes) are not communicated prior to the action, causing failure states.
cognitive_load_map: High: Dynamic Pricing ('Scaled by dates/sales'). User must map out future timelines and price jumps purely in their head.
High: Stock Control. User must understand the difference between ticket-level capacity and pool-level capacity.
Medium: Initial Wizard. Juggling event setup while simultaneously being forced to create an organizer profile and make fee structure decisions.
global_analysis.decision_quality: Variable. Users are well-supported when making simple text/content decisions, but are largely unsupported when making complex logistical decisions (dynamic pricing, stock linking). They are forced to make blind configurations without visual previews or 'what-if' calculators.
global_analysis.product_philosophy: Power over Simplicity. The product is designed to accommodate highly complex, edge-case event structures (multi-room festivals, tiered pricing, segregated stock). It achieves this by giving the user direct access to database parameters, sacrificing intuitive guidance and guardrails in favor of absolute flexibility.
